lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: In the parking lot, Maeve lifted the bottle and swallowed. Once. She capped it, put it in her bag, and walked back inside. Four years. Gone.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Reza sits with the closed chart under his palm. The handwriting in that entry is not his. The mistake in that entry is not his. His signature is at the bottom of the page.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He thinks about the intern he was — twenty-six, two months past the exam, running on borrowed sleep. The attending said: sign here, this reflects the record. He signed. He did not read closely enough to know what he was signing. He did not think there was anything to know.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Another version: he knew something was wrong. Not what. Not exactly. He felt the attending's hand on his shoulder, the weight of the chart pushed toward him, and he signed because the alternative was a question he was not sure how to ask.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He doesn't know. That is the actual problem. Not the error in the chart. Not the two years of proceedings. The thing he cannot answer — even now, alone in the break room at three a.m. — is whether he signed out of ignorance or out of deference. Whether it was a mistake or a choice.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: His name is at the bottom. It has been there for nine years.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He opens the chart again. Reads the entry one more time. The attending's handwriting, round and confident. His own signature, tidy, slightly rushed. He closes it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Someone else wrote the error. He still holds the pen. That is the only fact he is certain of. Everything else — what he knew, what he should have known, what kind of doctor he was and is — that is what the next six weeks are for.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He puts the chart in the return stack. Walks back to the board. Picks up the marker. The shift is not done.
